mnemonics.title: Tree Traversal Order
mnemonics.content: Preorder: 'Root First' (R-L-R), Inorder: 'In between' (L-Root-R), Postorder: 'Root Last' (L-R-Root). Remember: RL, LR, RR - think of letter positions.

mnemonics.title: BST Search Pattern
mnemonics.content: 'Go Left for Less, Go Right for More' - if search key < current node, go left; if > current node, go right. Stop when found or null encountered.

mnemonics.title: Full vs Complete Tree
mnemonics.content: 'Full is Full of Children' - every internal node has exactly 2 children. 'Complete fills from Left' - fill levels completely except last.

mnemonics.title: Spanning Tree Edges
mnemonics.content: 'Vertices minus One' - A tree with n vertices has exactly (n-1) edges. Therefore, a spanning tree of n-vertex graph has (n-1) edges.

acronyms.usage: Used for efficient search, insert, delete operations with O(log n) average case.
Self-balancing BST where balance factor = |height(left) - height(right)| ≤ 1 for all nodes.
Spanning tree with minimum total edge weight, used in network design, clustering.
Level-by-level traversal using queue; produces BFS spanning tree.
Explores as deep as possible before backtracking; used for preorder, inorder, postorder.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted tree (root at top). Height: measure from node DOWN to deepest leaf (like measuring tree's total height from top to ground). Depth: measure from root DOWN to node (like how deep underground a node is). Root has height = tree height, depth = 0.
Think of BST as organized bookshelf: All books (keys) smaller than current go to LEFT shelf, larger go to RIGHT shelf. Each shelf (node) has its own smaller left shelf and larger right shelf.

Draw tree and imagine 'walking': Preorder = stamp your foot at each node first (visit root), then go left, then right. Inorder = visit when 'between' left and right. Postorder = do everything else before stamping foot.

Imagine a city's road network as a graph. A spanning tree is like selecting minimum roads to ensure every location is reachable without any loops - like a subway map that connects all stations with no circles.

analogies.analogy: A linked list is like a train with only one connection between each car. A tree is like a family tree - each person (node) can have multiple children, but has only one parent (except the ancestor at top).

BST search is like searching a sorted phone book by opening to middle, deciding which half contains the name, then repeating. Each decision eliminates half the remaining options - same principle as binary search in arrays.

Root of a tree is like the CEO of a company - the single point from which all hierarchical relationships originate. Everyone reports to someone above them (parent), and managers may have multiple direct reports (children).

A balanced tree is like a well-organized company with minimal hierarchy levels. An unbalanced tree is like a company where one manager has all employees reporting directly - the 'height' becomes large, making it slow to find anyone.
